hero.tagline: New York's small claims courts offer a straightforward process for resolving monetary disputes up to $10,000, with varying limits based on court jurisdiction.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Limit varies by court: $10,000 in NYC Civil Court, $5,000 in City Courts outside NYC, $3,000 in Town and Village Courts.

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: $15 for claims up to $1,000; $20 for claims over $1,000.

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Small Claims Part of Civil Court
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by location: Civil Court in NYC, City Courts outside NYC, Town/Village Courts.

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Permitted but not required; small claims court is designed for self-representation.

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Must file a notice of appeal within 30 days of judgment.

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the past three years.

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds the court's monetary limit, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the cap. Instead, you must file in a higher court or waive the excess amount.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: New York allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed, with a statute of limitations of 6 years for written contracts and 6 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails, texts, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $1,500 with the understanding they'd repay in 3 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the loan terms and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 and failed to make payments as agreed.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from the due date. Present the original note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $3,000, invoiced the client, but haven't received payment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,500 on their account for goods delivered.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,000 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for services with a $1,200 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the bounced check and any bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $20 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $3,000 loan, the primary borrower defaulted, and you had to pay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Show the co-signed agreement and proof of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: New York's small claims courts handle various consumer disputes, with a 3-year statute of limitations for most cases.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $500 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from delivery. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $300 but left your home in poor condition.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,200 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory contractor work
claims.example: A contractor was paid $5,000 for a renovation but left the job incomplete.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid $800 for a DJ who never showed up at your event.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the service agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A magazine continued to charge you $50 annually after you canceled.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from charge. Show cancellation notice and bank statements.

claims.name: Defective used car purchase
claims.example: You bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales contract and any repair records.

claims.name: Misleading advertising
claims.example: A store advertised a product for $200 but charged you $300 at checkout.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from transaction. Provide the advertisement and receipt.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage in New York have a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $2,500 in damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Bring police reports and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor caused property damage
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell on your fence, causing $1,000 in damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant damaged rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $3,500 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Present the lease agreement and photos of damage.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog destroyed your $800 garden.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Provide photos and any witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: A dry cleaner ruined your $500 suit.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Bring the receipt and photos of damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: New York's small claims courts handle various landlord-tenant disputes, with specific statutes of limitations depending on the claim.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.

claims.name: No itemization of security deposit deductions
claims.example: Landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from lease termination. Request an itemized statement in writing.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat for weeks, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the conditions and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, the landlord kept your $2,000 worth of belongings.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. List the items and their value.

claims.name: Illegal eviction by landlord
claims.example: Landlord changed the locks without notice, barring you from your apartment.
claims.claim_statute: N.Y. Real Property Actions and Proceedings Law § 853
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from eviction. Report to local housing authority and document the incident.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but moved out early and the landlord kept the last month's rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease and proof of payment.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months' rent totaling $2,400.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You fixed a broken heater for $500 and deducted it from rent; landlord disputes this.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair. Keep all receipts and notify the landlord in writing before repairs.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: New York's small claims courts address various employment and wage disputes, with a 6-year statute of limitations for wage claims.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $1,000 for hours worked but not paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from non-payment. Provide timesheets and pay stubs.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer didn't issue your last paycheck of $800.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from non-payment. Document your resignation and any communication.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: You earned $2,500 in commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from non-payment. Show the commission agreement and sales records.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated accordingly.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from non-payment. Provide work schedules and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: New York's small claims courts handle various personal claims, with specific statutes of limitations depending on the claim.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store and incurred $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury. Bring medical records and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $600 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Document the loan agreement and requests for return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $1,500 bicycle without permission.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft. File a police report and provide evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: New York's small claims courts address various vehicle-related disputes, with specific statutes of limitations depending on the claim.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,000 for repairs you didn't authorize.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair orders and any communication.

claims.name: Vehicle wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, costing you $250.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from tow date. Provide photos of the parking area and tow receipt.

exclusions.explanation: Small Claims Court cannot grant possession of property; eviction proceedings must be filed in Housing Court.
Claims for defamation are not permitted in Small Claims Court as they do not involve monetary damages only.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdiction of Small Claims Court due to complexity and potential damages.
Issues like divorce, child support, and custody are outside the jurisdiction of Small Claims Court.
Small Claims Court lacks jurisdiction over federal entities; such claims must be filed in federal court.
Class action lawsuits are beyond the scope of Small Claims Court and must be filed in a higher court.
Small Claims Court can only award monetary damages, not compel actions or inactions.
Disputes involving property ownership are too complex for Small Claims Court.
Issues related to wills and estates are handled by Surrogate's Court, not Small Claims Court.
Claims for workplace injuries are under the jurisdiction of the Workers' Compensation Board.

damages.punitive_explanation: Small Claims Court in New York does not award punitive damages; it is limited to compensatory damages only.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $20 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in Small Claims Court unless specified by statute or contract.
damages.postjudgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 9% per annum from the date of judgment until paid.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Part of the Civil Court
where_to_file.parent_court: New York State Unified Court System

venue_rules.scenario: Defendant has a business in NYC
filing_options: County where defendant has a business
County where defendant resides, works, or has a business

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Electronic filing is not currently available for Small Claims Court in New York.

service_fees.notes: The court attempts service by certified mail; if unsuccessful, personal service is required.
If certified mail fails, the claimant must arrange and pay for personal service.

forms.name: Application for Waiver of Court Fees

eligibility_bullets: Income below 125% of the federal poverty level
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are not added to the judgment against the defendant.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment against the defendant.

entries.notes: Applies to contracts signed in writing.
Applies to contracts for the sale of goods.
Applies to written promises to pay money.
Applies to damage to personal or real property.
Applies to fraudulent acts; discovery rule applies.
Applies to checks returned for insufficient funds.
Applies to claims for unpaid compensation.
Applies to recovery of security deposits.
Applies to actions to enforce judgments.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or due to bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, a written demand for payment must be sent, and if unpaid after 30 days, you may seek damages up to twice the check amount.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but documenting requests for payment is advisable.
pre_filing.government_claim_notes: Claims against municipalities require a notice of claim to be filed within 90 days of the incident.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: For security deposit disputes, it's recommended to request the return in writing before filing a claim.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: For deceptive practices, a written demand for relief is advisable before filing under consumer protection statutes.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim and amount sought.

forms.name: Notice of Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to assert a counterclaim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Application for Waiver of Court Fees
forms.description: Requests a waiver of filing and service fees based on financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to initiate an appeal of the small claims court's decision.

methods.name: Certified mail by court clerk
methods.description: The court attempts to serve the defendant by sending the notice via certified mail.
cons: May fail if defendant doesn't accept mail

methods.name: Personal service by process server
methods.description: A professional process server delivers the notice directly to the defendant.

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff
methods.description: The sheriff's office delivers the notice to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, you may request permission from the court for alternative service methods.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document your attempts and request alternative service from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear in court on the hearing date.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the denial within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to a higher court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial, typically lasting 15-30 minutes.
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk prior to the hearing date.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 30 days.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation services to help parties settle before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The appellate court reviews the record for legal errors; no new evidence is considered.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but it's advisable to first file a motion to vacate.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 9% per annum from the date of judgment until paid.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File an income execution with the court, serve it on the debtor's employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 90% of earnings or 30 times the minimum wage per week, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a restraining notice and levy from the court, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank will freeze and turn over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.
exemptions: $2,640 in a bank account is exempt from levy.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a transcript of judgment with the county clerk where the debtor owns property, creating a lien that must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.
exemptions: Homestead exemption up to $150,000 in NYC.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize non-exempt property, and sell it at auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.
exemptions: Personal property exemptions vary; consult state statutes.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a renewal affidavit with the court before the 20-year period expires to extend the judgment.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized by filing date; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Corporations and partnerships must be represented by an attorney in Small Claims Court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must sue and be sued under their legal names; trade names are insufficient.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves but cannot send an employee in their place.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from suing for unpaid work if a license is required.

county_variations.detail: Small Claims Court in NYC has a monetary limit of $10,000.
Small Claims Court in Nassau County has a monetary limit of $5,000.
Small Claims Court in Suffolk County has a monetary limit of $5,000.
Town and Village Courts have a monetary limit of $3,000.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue the wrong party or use incorrect information, your case may be dismissed or the judgment unenforceable.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the defendant's legal name and address before filing; for businesses, check the Department of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding monetary limits
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing a claim exceeding the court's monetary limit can result in dismissal or the need to waive the excess amount.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim amount is within the appropriate limit for the court where you're filing.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the court may not hear your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for serving the defendant, and ensure proof of service is filed.

pitfalls.title: Lack of evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Without sufficient evidence, you may lose your case even if you're in the right.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you don't prepare, you could owe them money.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Review any counterclaims carefully and prepare your defense accordingly.

recent_changes.title: Increase in monetary limit for NYC Small Claims Court
recent_changes.description: The monetary limit for Small Claims Court in New York City increased from $5,000 to $10,000, allowing for higher-value claims to be filed.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a lawyer to file or defend a case in Small Claims Court; the process is designed for individuals to represent themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but most cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In New York City, the maximum is $10,000; in City Courts outside NYC, it's $5,000; and in Town and Village Courts, it's $3,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in your favor, but you must still prove your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal the court's decision within 30 days by filing a notice of appeal and paying the required fee.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment is valid for 20 years in New York, and you can take steps to enforce it during that time.

state_specific_notes: New York allows individuals to file small claims cases without an attorney, promoting self-representation.
The monetary limits for small claims vary by court jurisdiction, with NYC allowing up to $10,000.
Small Claims Court in New York is designed to be informal and accessible, with simplified procedures.
Corporations and partnerships must be represented by an attorney in Small Claims Court.
Electronic filing is not currently available for Small Claims Court cases in New York.

interesting_findings: New York's Small Claims Court offers evening sessions to accommodate individuals who cannot attend during regular business hours.
The court attempts to serve the defendant by certified mail, reducing the burden on the plaintiff to arrange service.
If the defendant cannot be served by mail, the court provides guidance on alternative service methods.
Small Claims Court judgments accrue interest at 9% per annum until paid.
The court provides resources and guides to assist individuals in navigating the small claims process without legal representation.

sources.label: New York State Unified Court System — Small Claims Handbook
